entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
In a recent discussion on the Bitcoin protocol, the challenge of maintaining the blockchain's security without compromising its foundational principles was highlighted. The debate revolves around three main approaches to address the security funding issue as the block rewards diminish: significantly increasing block sizes, surpassing the 21 million Bitcoin cap, or relying predominantly on miner fees from merged mining. The latter, which is detailed in a post ([Security Budget II: Merged Mining](https://www.truthcoin.info/blog/security-budget-ii-mm/)), appears to be the most feasible option according to current discussions. This method is already utilized by RSK and could potentially be implemented through a soft fork.
The conversation also delves into the inherent value of Bitcoin, emphasizing that scarcity alone does not confer value unless the asset is also useful. This utility is largely derived from the robust cryptographic measures that secure the network. If these measures fail to efficiently balance security with cost, Bitcoin could either become too expensive (over-secured) or vulnerable (under-secured) compared to potential competitors. This imbalance underscores the lack of a feedback mechanism within the Bitcoin system to determine the optimal level of security needed.
Furthermore, there's a proposal to adjust the security budget through a novel approach that involves modifying the payout structure for miners via a soft fork. This would lock away part of the mining rewards for future distribution, potentially extending the security budget over several more decades. This concept echoes a presentation by Anthony Towns at a previous Scaling Bitcoin conference, suggesting a strategic delay in reward payouts to manage long-term security funding. This technique, while not a permanent solution, could provide a temporary respite and delay the depletion of the security budget, allowing for more sustainable management of the blockchain’s integrity.
